data.title: Endesa, S.A. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Endesa, S.A. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Amazon's Operating Margins Continue To Improve
data.description:
Introduction

Per my February article, Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) employees are getting more productive. The 1Q25 numbers have come out since then and there are positive developments including continued progress with seasonally adjusted operating margins.

My thesis is that Amazon’s operating margins continue to improve.

The Numbers

Per comments from CFO Brian Olsavsky in the 1Q25 call, there were one-time charges for the period impacting the North America and international segments. These charges hurt operating margins for the segments by 90 basis points and 70 basis points, respectively. These charges were related to historical returns and costs to receive inventory which was pulled forward because of expected tariffs.

North American operating margin for the 1Q25 period was 6.3%, and it would have been 7.2% were it not for the one-time charges mentioned above. For comparison, North American operating margin for 1Q24 was 5.8%. International operating margin for 1Q25 was 3%, and it would have been 3.7% were it not for the one-time charges. Looking at the 1Q24 period from a year ago, operating margin for international was 2.8%.

CFO Olsavsky went on in the 1Q25 call to explain factors leading to improved margins (emphasis added):

In Q1, our newly re-architected inbound network drove productivity in our fulfillment and transportation network, leading to better inventory placement and higher units per package, and as a result, lower delivery costs. Beyond Q1, we have a number of initiatives underway to continue improving our cost structure, such as fine-tuning our inbound network, building out our same-day delivery sites, expanding our rural delivery network, and adding robotics and automation to our facilities. Better inventory placement remains a top priority. Better placement drives more in-stock selection, reduces travel distances, and speeds up delivery. And having inventory in the right place at the right time increases the likelihood that multiple items can be combined in a package, which helps reduce packaging and cost. Although progress won't always be linear, we have a good plan to continue to drive improvement over time.

The above comments about lowering delivery costs are important moving forward because fulfillment increased more than sales from 1Q24 to 1Q25. In other words, the Y/Y change for fulfillment was 10.2% from 1Q24 to 1Q25 while the Y/Y change for sales was only 8.6% ($ in millions):

One of the most impactful numbers above is the cost of sales line, and it was nice to see this only increased 6% Y/Y. I think this can continue to become a smaller percentage of sales as Amazon keeps fine-tuning their inbound network. It is stressful seeing the technology and infrastructure increase at a higher rate than net sales, but much of this is for AWS where the Y/Y sales growth is higher than the overall Y/Y sales growth. I like the way the 1Q25 release breaks out shipping and stock-based compensation (“SBC”). Shipping grew by a mere 3% Y/Y from $21.8 billion in 1Q24 to $22.5 in 1Q25. SBC actually shrank 26% Y/Y and the biggest part of this hits the technology and infrastructure line:

It’s nice seeing the margin improvements in both AWS and non-AWS segments:

Again, the above improvements are even more impressive when we take into account the comments from the 1Q25 call which revealed 1Q25 margins for North America and international would have been 7.2% and 3.7%, respectively, were it not for one-time charges.

Valuation

The increases we’re seeing in capex are concerning and there is risk here. If management miscalculates such that the depreciation and amortization are understated, then accrual earnings are no longer a good proxy for the amount of cash which can be pulled out of the business. And of course, a company is worth the amount of cash we can pull out of it from now until judgment day. In 1Q24, the depreciation and amortization of $11,684 million was 78% of the gross capex of $14,925 million at the time. Things are different now. In 1Q25, the depreciation and amortization of $14,262 million is only 57% of the gross capex of $25,019 million. I believe CEO Andy Jassy when he says the AI capex investments are the opportunity of a lifetime, but part of me still has concerns.

Another valuation risk concern is the slowing of the non-AWS growth rate. Historically, sales for the first quarter is always lower than the preceding seasonal fourth quarter, but the first quarter numbers have held up nicely against the previous third quarter numbers until now. This is easy to see in a graph where the 1P and 3P volume numbers for 3Q24 are greater than they were for 1Q25:

These are some of the other numbers I consider for valuation:

We need to think about overall EPS growth, its drivers, cash flows and new factors such as tariffs. As we said above, there are concerns with some non-AWS considerations such as 1P and 3P sales growth. If we take out the seasonal fourth quarter, we see these numbers shrank instead of growing from 3Q24 to 1Q25. Additionally, these segments only grew 5% and 6%, respectively, Y/Y. The advertising business is also non-AWS, and it grew nicely at 18% Y/Y, but it also struggled on a quarterly basis, falling from 3Q24 to 1Q25. Still, from an overall perspective, AWS is more important for EPS growth and AWS did well in terms of growth both Y/Y and Q/Q (no seasonality adjustment is needed here). I believe AWS will continue to drive overall EPS growth in the quarters and years ahead. Again, the obvious concern here is the mismatch between cash flow and accrual earnings as AWS continues to become a more important part of accrual earnings. Tariffs can impact both retail and AWS. On the retail side, I’m optimistic 3P sellers can make adjustments to keep volume moving. On the AWS side, I think demand will remain strong for compute all over the world despite the changing landscape we’re seeing with tariffs.

In my last article, I was optimistic non-AWS operating margins could eventually reach 7%, but now I feel even better about the prospects and I think they could one day be 8%. Trailing twelve months (“TTM”) operating income for non-AWS segments is $538,527 million. If we use a hypothetical 8% margin, then the hypothetical operating earning power is $43 billion. In the past, I’ve used a 35 to 40x multiple on this, but now I think that might be a bit ambitious. If we use a multiple of 30 to 35x instead, then it gives us a non-AWS valuation range of $1,290 to $1,505 billion.

AWS has 1Q25 operating income of $11,547 million, which gives us a run rate of $46.2 billion. The segment continues to grow nicely, and it benefits from being the first hyperscaler such that it has a higher margin than competitors like Google Cloud even when sales are similar. In other words, Google Cloud had sales of $12.3 billion in 1Q25 and their operating margin was 17.5%, but AWS had a margin of 28.3% back in the 4Q20 period when their sales were $12.7 billion:

In the past, I’ve used a multiple of 25 to 30x for AWS and this is lower than the multiple for non-AWS, in part because I worry about the heavy capex requirements for AWS. Applying the multiple gives us a valuation range of $1,155 to $1,385 billion when rounding to the nearest $5 billion. I’m trying to be conservative and if the depreciation figures are accurate, then AWS probably has a higher valuation range than what I’ve shown here.

Summing up, my valuation range is as follows:

$1,290 to $1,505 billion non-AWS

$1,155 to $1,385 AWS

—-----------------------------------------

$2,445 to $2,890 billion

The 1Q25 10-Q shows 10,616,352,407 shares as of April 23. Multiplying by the May 7 share price of $188.71 gives us a market cap of $2 trillion. The market cap is below my valuation range and I think the stock is a buy for long-term investors who will hold it for at least three years.

Forward-looking investors should monitor the capex spend in upcoming quarters and scrutinize assumptions with depreciation and amortization.

Disclaimer: Any material in this article should not be relied on as a formal investment recommendation. Never buy a stock without doing your own thorough research.

data.title: The U.K. Is Relying On EU Trade Reset To Bolster Its Public Finances
data.description:
By James Smith

The UK is seeking to secure a number of deals

Trade deals are like buses. You don’t see one for weeks, then two – maybe even three – come along at once.

The UK and India have, after years of talks, agreed a deal that will reduce tariffs for some UK-made goods in exchange for tax concessions on Indian workers moving to Britain, amongst other things. And if reports are accurate, the UK has virtually reached a deal with the US on exempting at least a portion of British steel and car exports from President Trump’s sectoral tariffs. Time will tell whether that gets signed.

The UK is walking a fine line on both sets of negotiations, cautious not to give concessions that could jeopardise talks with the European Union, which could prove much more consequential.

This is not simply about reducing reliance on the US. All of these negotiations, particularly those with the EU, are designed above all to ease the pressure on Britain’s public finances.

The logic is simple. The Office for Budget Responsibility, which polices the fiscal rules, judged that Brexit would generate a permanent 4% hit to productivity. Lower productivity means slower economic growth. And slower economic growth makes it harder to reduce budget deficits and stabilise debt-to-GDP ratios, which is what the UK’s fiscal rules mandate.

UK trade with the EU is more significant than with the US

Closer UK-EU ties could be helpful for the public finances

Closer EU trades will, the UK government hopes, convince the OBR that some of this productivity hit can be undone.

Formally, the UK has said it doesn’t want to regain single market or customs union membership. But those red lines could get significantly watered down over the coming months.

The government has said it wants to agree to a veterinary agreement, which would see Britain realign with EU food standards and remove cumbersome border checks. In practice, we think that an agreement could cover a much wider range of goods exports. The UK hasn’t meaningfully diverged from EU product regulation since Brexit, after all.

Of course, the EU would have to agree, and for some in Brussels, this probably sounds a lot like cherry-picking. Then again, the political climate has changed dramatically since the UK left the EU in 2020. Migration isn’t the priority it once was, while the UK has a strong hand to play on defence.

Both sides meet for a summit on 19 May, and positive headlines could potentially have a tangible impact on UK markets, even if those initial talks are centred on military rather than economic matters.

Whether this is a game-changer for the UK’s public finances, we’re not so sure. That 4% productivity hit to the OBR's forecasts is not going to be fully regained, given the UK isn't going to completely re-align with the EU. And that limits the scope for upward revisions to those OBR growth projections, even if a deal can be reached before the Autumn budget.

In fact, given the more sombre global economic outlook, it’s more likely those forecasts will get revised down. And that makes further tax rises look increasingly unavoidable later this year.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Lower U.S. Consumer Spending Can Already Be Seen
data.description:
As of March 2025, a meaningful slice of US consumers reported spending less at retail stores and online compared to last year. Some Americans are cutting back their restaurant dining, cinema attendance and even online video subscriptions.

The S&P Global Market Intelligence Kagan first quarter 2025 Consumer Insights survey asked respondents to assess how their behavior in 2025 has compared to last year. For instance, 29% of total internet adults said they were spending less this year at retail stores, compared to only 12% citing increased year-over-year spending.

With regard to online purchases, 23% said they were spending less in 2025, while 20% reported a higher level of spending this year. Over one-third (36%) indicated they were dining out at restaurants less frequently this year, more than double the 14% that have increased their restaurant visits.

Among those spending more at retail stores in 2025 than last year, nearly half (47%) were young adults, ages 18-34. Older adults, aged 55 and above constitute approximately one-quarter (24%) of those with increased retail spending this year. Those spending less at retail stores in 2025 were slightly skewed toward older 55+ adults (41%) with 32% in the 18-34 age bracket.

The 2025 age distribution among those spending more or less from online sources appears to be more uniform than for retail store spending. Approximately four in 10 (39%) of those indicating increased online spending this year were older 55+ adults; very similar to the 36% of younger 18-34 year-olds who expected higher online spending in 2025. The age distribution for those spending less online in 2025 closely mirrored that of the increased online spenders.

When it comes to dining out at restaurants, the survey data shows that older Americans tend to be cutting back, while younger adults are eating out more frequently. The data reveals that 43% of those who said they are spending less at restaurants in 2025 were adults aged 55 and older. Similarly, 43% of those with increased restaurant visits year over year were young adults (18-34).

When US consumers cut their retail store and online spending, which retailers will continue to get their money? To assess this, the following chart depicts the percentage point variance between the retailers used by those with reduced spending in 2025 and the overall survey.

For example, the survey found that 32% of those with reduced 2025 spending shop at the Home Depot (HD), compared to 37% of the total survey sample, yielding a negative five percentage point variance. Reduced consumer spending has the potential to impact nearly all retailers, ranging from e-commerce giants like Amazon (AMZN) to big box retailers (Target (TGT) and Costco (COST)), drug stores and the home improvement sector.

The entertainment sector is not immune to economic pressures that reduce consumer spending. The survey found that 27% of US internet adults have fewer online video subscriptions (SVOD) in 2025 than last year, while 16% have subscribed to additional SVOD services.

Over one-third (35%) said they have been attending the cinema less in 2025, more than three times (11%) those that are seeing more movies this year. Time spent watching free online video appears to be fairly stable with 21% having more viewing hours in 2025, compared to 18% with fewer viewing hours.

Among those spending less at retail stores or online in 2025, 58% said they were also attending the cinema less often, and approximately half (47%) have fewer SVOD subscriptions than last year.

Yet, 36% of these reduced spenders said they also are spending less time watching free online video, significantly more than the 26% that reported having more free online video viewing hours this year. This suggests that free online video services will not necessarily benefit greatly from consumers spending less on SVOD services and in-theater movies.

Original Post

data.title: METU Does What It Says On The Tin, 200% META Stock
data.description:
Introduction

It's been easy to be a Meta Platforms (META) bull, so much so that it was included in both the FANG and Mag 7 groupings of stocks, and has been on a multi-year run that has outperformed the broader Mag 7 group and the S&P 500 over the last few years, by significant margins.

This has brought a lot of attention to META, and has been encouraging for investors interested in accessing leverage for META stock.

After my review of PLTU, a fund that provides leverage on Palantir (PLTR) stock, I began to look through the rest of fund manager Direxion's catalog. Immediately, I wanted to review the Direxion Daily META Bull 2X Shares (NASDAQ:METU), which aims to provide, according to the Direxion website:

...daily investment results, before fees and expenses, of 200%...of the performance of the common shares of Meta Platforms, Inc.

Before we proceed any further with this METU review, any time I introduce leveraged investments, it's important to cover the major risks involved with these kinds of assets.

Warning: Major Risks Ahead

There are three warnings that I think investors should consider when looking at any daily-resetting leveraged fund:

1) The SEC notes:

Leveraged and inverse ETFs typically are designed to achieve their stated performance objectives on a daily basis. Some investors might invest in these ETFs with the expectation that the ETFs may meet their stated daily performance objectives over the long term as well. Investors should be aware that the performance of these ETFs over a period longer than one day can differ significantly from their stated daily performance objectives and may potentially expose investors to significant and sudden losses.

2) The fund's prospectus notes:

The Funds seek daily leveraged investment results and are intended to be used as short-term trading vehicles. Each Fund attempts to provide daily investment results that correspond to 2 times the performance of an underlying security. The Funds are not intended to be used by, and are not appropriate for, investors who do not intend to actively monitor and manage their portfolios...

The Funds are not suitable for all investors. The Funds are designed to be utilized only by sophisticated investors, such as traders and active investors employing dynamic strategies...

If a Fund's underlying security moves more than 50% on a given trading day in a direction adverse to the Fund, the Fund's investors would lose all of their money.

3) This review of MSTX, the "Case Study For Bad Leveraged ETFs" as a cautionary tale.

META vs. METU

The way that these funds operate is that they reset daily, meaning every day, investors should get 2x the performance of META stock on that day. Because it is not long-term leverage, this means that moves are outsized in both directions, since METU also falls 2x the amount META down on bad days.

Despite at one point being up over 80% from its inception, METU is now underperforming META as a long-term holding. This is what makes it not suitable for investors to buy and hold. It went from being up over 80% to being down more than 20% in a not-so-short amount of time.

These swings can be too much for the psychology of many investors, even if they have a plan to use METU as a non-day trading tool. Consider also the warning in the prospectus about the 50% decline in META meaning certain destruction for the fund.

While this hasn't happened in META's history, it does hold the record for the last one-day wipe out of wealth in a single stock, at a whopping $251B shaved off the market cap that day. It was a 22% drop by the end, and would have cut METU nearly in half had it been around back then.

Those kinds of quick and sudden drops are devastating for these kinds of leveraged funds like METU, and the reason investors may not be better off holding them, or even trading them, than the actual stock. We can take a look at the most recent fall on April 4th, which METU was around for, to see how negatively sudden drops (and it wasn't META's fault this time!) can really erode investor gains.

The loss was so sharp that even when META spiked the following week and recovered, METU was still at a loss. Subsequent slow, grinding lower days on META following the recovery tanked METU far below the April 4th crash.

METU Holdings & Strategy

The actual holdings in the fund are fairly straightforward. Here is the full holdings list for METU:

There are several components that we can see: the cash component, total return swaps on META stock, and META stock itself, which is to ensure that the fund doesn't go to zero in a 50% drop. That 10% allocation to META stock will buoy the fund, and it should only lose 90% of its NAV.

The fund's prospectus notes this as part of the adviser's plan to ensure there can't be a full one-day wipe out of the fund, although I would personally consider losing 90% of my investment a wipe out.

The Funds' investment adviser...Will attempt to position each Fund's portfolio to ensure that a Fund does not gain or lose more than 90% of its net asset value on a given trading day. As a consequence, a Fund's portfolio should not be responsive to underlying security movements beyond 45% on a given trading day, whether that movement is favorable or adverse to the Fund.

On the flip side, that means the fund won't gain linearly on a day when META jumps over 45%, not that that has ever happened, and likely never will. META's largest daily gain on record was back in February 2024, which is one of the largest daily gains of all time, and still only comes in at 20%.

Suitability

I am a believer that leveraged ETFs can be useful tools, but not great long-term investments. This is especially true for those that reset daily, and for those that focus on a single stock like METU does.

META on its own can be very volatile; in this article, we discussed that it held records (still does for losses, but is second to NVDA for gains) for intraday volatility. Many investors may find that META on its own is already pushing their risk tolerance, let alone a leveraged position.

However, there is a use case for METU speculating on events like earnings reports where META could run up, making METU sprint.

Employ heavy caution when making these trades, monitor positions closely, and consider allocating no more than 2% of a speculative portfolio to this fund at the most; single stock leverage is not to be trifled with. It should also be noted that investors should avoid holding this fund overnight, as the risk of a catalyst making the stock move dramatically during a time when you cannot trade could result in horrific losses (or gains, depending on the catalyst).

Conclusion

The Direxion Daily META Bull 2X Shares is a fund made for trading, but that's not inherently a bad thing. For what it is, Direxion does a great job, and the fund seems to track Meta Platforms stock very well, offering daily 2x leverage with few errors that appeared in its short lifetime.

It's important for traders to note the tools at our disposal, and which ones are useful. Some of them are not, and most leveraged funds I review, I am not impressed with. METU is one exception, as its construction and performance show that the fund is being run with prudence and risk management in mind (at least, in the scope of leveraged ETFs), with the managers building in support to ensure the fund cannot be wiped out in a single day.

Engage with this fund at your own peril, but know that it should achieve its desired results, 200% performance of META on a given day, up to a ±45% move in META, based on the data we have for it.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Verisk Analytics: Rating Downgrade As Valuation Multiple Climbs
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Verisk Analytics (NASDAQ:VRSK) was a buy rating because I believed the strong growth outlook could support the valuation premium, and the upside was attractive. The solid 1Q25 results validated my thesis, which helps me to stay convinced in the business's fundamentals. However, with the significant run-up in valuation to ~43x forward PE, the risk/reward profile has shifted. The current multiple limits near-term upside, and hence, I am downgrading to a hold rating.

1Q25 results update: Subscription revenue continues to be the main driver

VRSK 1Q25 was a solid start to FY25. Total revenue grew 7.9% on an organic basis to $753 million, which was a 100 bps acceleration vs. 1Q24, suggesting that underlying demand is still healthy. This growth performance was also ahead of my FY25 assumption of 7% growth. What was really encouraging regarding the topline performance is the strength in subscription revenue. If you recall my previous writeup, this has been a big part of my bull case, and 1Q25 results showed that momentum isn't slowing down. Per the earnings release, 83% of insurance revenue now comes from hosted subscriptions-up from 80% in 1Q24. That mix shift matters a lot because it makes the business model more stable and predictable. Growth was solid too: subscription revenue grew 10.6% y/y on an organic basis.

Margins also did well. Gross margin expanded by 170 bps y/y, which drove adj. EBITDA margin up to 55.3% (130 bps y/y expansion). While adj. net came down a little, this was primarily due to the difference in tax rate. Adj. EPS still managed to grow ~7%, up to $1.73 for 1Q25. More importantly, management is sticking with its FY25 outlook: revenue of $3.03-3.08 billion, adj. EBITDA of $1.67-1.72 billion (implying 55-56% margins), and EPS of $6.80-7.10. This is good, especially in what I would say is a very uncertain macro backdrop.

Value-Based Pricing and GTM Enhancements Bearing Fruit

In my last write-up, I talked about VRSK pivoting to value-based pricing and its new go-to-market [GTM] strategy as key levers for growth. In the 1Q25 results, VRSK has proven that these initiatives are working. Management clearly pointed out strong price realization as a driver behind the subscription growth, which to me is a clear sign that the value-based pricing approach is working. This also tells us that VRSK is increasingly moving away from having its revenue growth being purely tied to client premium volumes.

The GTM restructuring I highlighted previously is turning out to be the right move as I expected, and I inferred this from management's commentary around better engagement:

"Continued to enhance client engagement through our refined go-to-market strategy, resulting in deeper relationships and a better understanding of client needs across the enterprise…" 1Q25 press release

I have touched briefly on the positive impacts of this GTM change, but I now think a strong flywheel effect may start to kick in. Here is how I see it. A better alignment (the sales team having the right knowledge and incentives) drives deeper customer conversations, and this leads to stronger pricing power (offering the right product) and stickier relationships (more products adopted means VRSK is more embedded in the workflow), and this gives VRSK more financial capacity to reinvest into better GTM alignment.

Product Innovation and Acquisition of Simplitium

Finally, regarding the last growth lever I mentioned before-product innovation-it is also working very well. And the part that convinces me is that it is driving P&L performance. Although we do not know the direct contribution, it was noted that underwriting revenue growth was driven by model and content enhancements (source: VRSK 10-Q). What reinforces my bullish view is that VRSK is enhancing its proprietary data. As I have said before, having the right data is key to enabling better decision-making. VRSK's April acquisition of Simplitium does this perfectly. This is more than just a model-count improvement; I think it is a smart, strategic move to expand the VRSK risk platform as it layers on >300 third-party models. This significantly broadens the perspectives that clients can view risk, especially for clients with specialized or emerging risk needs (i.e., beyond the scope of what VRSK originally has).

This acquisition also supports VRSK expansion plans in Europe, and I think the implications here are more significant than the headline suggests. By acquiring Simplitium, VRSK now has a faster time-to-market, be it through an existing customer base or through access to models that are already tailored for European regulatory frameworks and risk categories. That makes the VRSK platform immediately more relevant to local insurers. On top of that, it also equips VRSK with local expertise. I think this really deserves a highlight, as local market knowledge and relationships matter a lot when you're trying to scale in a new market.

Valuation

The only problem here is that VRSK valuation has run a lot higher than I expected. While the share price has yet to meet my original target price of $324, valuation has gone right up to ~43x forward PE. I don't think VRSK is worth >40x given its growth outlook. For comparison, similar peers in the data solution industry like S&P Global, MSCI, and Moody's Corp. only trade in the ~30x forward PE range. My view is that because of the potential for growth acceleration (as all the growth levers continue to get pulled, which VRSK is executing well), it should trade higher than its historical average (my base cash model has it at +1 standard deviation-36x).

Keeping my assumptions the same-acceleration to 12% revenue growth and modest margin expansion to 34% by FY27, and multiples rerate to 36x-I see an 8% upside from here. As much as I like to think the bull case can hold (multiples stay at >40x), the base case upside is not as attractive anymore. There is also a risk that multiples can get contracted fast in the near term if growth disappoints. As such, I downgraded to a hold rating.

Conclusion

My view for VRSK is now a hold rating. While the business continues to execute well across its strategic initiatives, the current valuation appears to fully reflect these positives. The limited upside in my base case, coupled with the risk of multiple contractions should growth expectations moderate, suggests that staying on the sidelines is a better move today.

data.title: Bullhorn Bounce
data.description:
Wall Street did as instructed when President Trump told investors to “go out and buy stock now.” Thursday’s rally wasn’t so much a policy endorsement as a standing ovation for lower tariffs - with a familiar showman working the tent.

Level Change 5/8/25 (%)

+0.6 Dow

+1.1 Nasdaq

+1.0 Nasdaq 100

+0.6 S&P 500

+1.2 S&P 400

+1.8 S&P 600

Indexes extended Wednesday’s modest gains, fueled by talk of tariff relief and the first deal breakthrough, with the UK. Washington and London announced a “framework agreement,” prompting the president to dust off his megaphone and tell traders to buy stocks immediately.

The bigger ticket was China. Beijing strongman Xi Jinping “very much wants to make a deal,” according to the president, who said he might hop on a call if weekend talks in Switzerland go well.

He floated the idea of slashing China's tariffs from 145% to a cuddlier 50–54% as early as next week. It’s the kind of fuzzy optimism investors now crave, a volatile de-escalation loop complete with its own campaign slogan: We Make Things Worse to Make Them Better Again.

Behind the scenes, systematic strategies reportedly re-leveraged into the rally, with resilient retail buyers and corporate buybacks doing their part. No major movement on the budget standoff, though Trump did dangle a plan to hike the top tax rate to 39.6%.

Between his tariff tax hikes and pending income tax hike, the Commander-in-Household-Budget-Destruction is defying all political convention. Raising taxes has long been considered electoral poison, but Trump looks intent on testing his 2016 theory - that he could shoot someone on Fifth Avenue and not lose any voters - on kitchen-table accounting: I can blow up household budgets coast-to-coast and not lose any voters. Test in progress.

In macro land, the Fed’s decision Wednesday to hold rates at 4.25–4.50% surprised no one. The statement was more interesting, acknowledging growing risks to both inflation and employment. Chair Powell’s press conference walked a traditional tightrope to non-commitment.

The Fed sees little harm in waiting, he said, and plenty in guessing wrong. Asked which half of the dual mandate might falter first, Powell essentially shrugged. When a ship’s taking on water at both bow and stern, debating which end hits the seabed first feels academic.

That uncertainty echoed in fresh data.

Initial jobless claims fell to 228K, matching forecasts, but continuing claims came in hot. Q1 productivity dropped more than expected, and unit labor costs surged at their fastest pace in a year. A mixed bag that won’t invite sweet dreams at the Eccles Building.

The New York Fed’s April survey showed a slip in 5-year inflation expectations, but the 3-year rose to its highest since July 2022. Households are growing gloomier about jobs and finances, and inflation expectations remain sticky, just like the Fed’s policy dilemma.

One bright spot: earnings keep humming.

Nearly 90% of the S&P 500 has reported, with earnings growth tracking around 13.5%, up from just 7.2% at the quarter’s start. About 77% have topped estimates, with upside surprises averaging more than 8.5%. Most firms are busy sidestepping tariffs and muting macro noise. Resilience is the theme; relief, the wish.

Thursday’s message? Stocks can rally while confusion reigns - especially if a ringmaster in a red tie shouts “Buy!” loud enough.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tariff Uncertainty Could Push Back Fed Rate Moves To Late 2025
data.description:
Uncertainty about the timing and impact of US President Donald Trump's tariffs led Federal Reserve officials to hold interest rates steady for a potentially longer-than-expected period.

The Fed's rate-setting Federal Open Market Committee voted May 7 to maintain the central bank's benchmark rate within its current target of 4.25% to 4.5%, where it has been since the Fed last cut rates by 25 basis points in December 2024.

Previously, Fed officials anticipated rate cuts throughout the year. But Trump's plan to impose tariffs on nearly all US trading partners has altered that outlook. The Fed is unlikely to adjust rates until the economic effects of the tariffs become evident in data, particularly concerning inflation and the domestic job market.

"It's really appropriate, we think, for us to be patient and wait for things to unfold as we get more clarity about what we should do," Fed Chairman Jerome Powell said during a press conference following the announcement that the Fed would keep rates unchanged.

Powell said the US economy's relative health allows for patience on rates. The unemployment rate has hovered around 4% over the past year and inflation has moderated, showing progress toward the Fed's 2% target.

However, Trump's tariffs are the key unknown factor, Powell said. The Fed's future actions and timing depend almost entirely on the tariffs' scale, scope, timing and duration.

"The right thing to do is to await further clarity," Powell said.

Wait and see

Given the timing of Fed meetings, government data releases and a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs expected to last until early July, the full impact of tariffs on inflation and jobs may not appear in data until the fall, indicating that clarity could still be months away.

Scott Anderson, chief US economist and managing director at BMO Capital Markets, said the Fed's "wait and see" approach is likely the best option due to the increased uncertainty surrounding future inflation and the labor market.

"Move too early in the wrong direction on rates and you create a serious monetary policy error that could put the financial markets into a tailspin," Anderson said.

Following Powell's press conference, about 73% of the futures market predicted the Fed would cut rates by at least 0.25 percentage point by July, according to CME FedWatch. A month prior, nearly 100% of the market anticipated at least one cut by July.

Now, almost all the market expects at least one cut by September. But Powell said the Fed's next move will likely depend on how tariffs affect inflation, economic growth and unemployment.

"The Fed is acknowledging that uncertainty has increased with more upside risk for both inflation and unemployment," said James Knightley, chief international economist at ING. "This suggests little inclination to move until they are confident of the direction the data is heading, meaning rate cuts could be delayed but risk being sharper when they come."

Powell did not exclude the possibility that the first rate move after the tariffs could be an increase if inflation rises significantly. Core personal consumption expenditures, excluding food and energy, could rise to 4% by the fourth quarter due to tariffs, up from 2.6% in March, BMO's Anderson said.

Tariffs have increased uncertainty in financial markets, which could continue for months as the Fed maintains its monetary policy, said Bill Zox, a portfolio manager at Brandywine Global.

"If the financial markets don't push for more clarity, it could take some time to flesh out the tariffs and the impact on the real economy," Zox said. "If a recession becomes imminent, the Fed may be late and may have to catch up with large rate cuts."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: FDIS: Caution Signs Appearing In The Consumer Discretionary Sector
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Fidelity MSCI Consumer Discretionary Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FDIS) warrants a hold rating due to multiple concerning outlook factors for the fund. While tariff and recession risks loom over FDIS's top holdings, there are also other fundamental concerns such as declining free cash flow, reduced sales, and high valuations for the ETF's top-weighted companies.

Fidelity MSCI Consumer Discretionary Index ETF - Overview and Compared ETFs

FDIS is an ETF that seeks to capture the performance of the consumer discretionary sector of the U.S. stock market. With its inception in 2013, the fund has 262 holdings and $1.72B in AUM. Consumer discretionary, as opposed to consumer staples, is a category broadly defined as goods and services that are desirable, but not essential. This includes industries such as retail, luxury goods, hotels, restaurants, and automobiles.

For comparison purposes, other funds examined are Vanguard Consumer Discretionary ETF (VCR), Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Consumer Discretionary ETF (RSPD), and Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY). VCR is another passively managed ETF that seeks to capture the consumer discretionary sector but tracks a different index than FDIS. RSPD equally weighs companies that are part of the consumer discretionary sector and therefore predominantly consists of mid-cap holdings. XLY seeks to capture the consumer discretionary sector of the S&P 500 Index.

FDIS: Performance, Expense Ratio, and Dividend Yield

FDIS has seen a strong 10-year CAGR of 11.89%. This return is roughly on par with VCR and XLY with 10-year CAGRs of 11.72% and 11.36%, respectively. Due to generally favorable economic conditions, FDIS and cap-weighted peer funds have performed well compared to the broader U.S. market over the past decade. Equal-weighted RSPD significantly underperformed, with a 10-year return of 6.37%.

A strong advantage for FDIS is its low expense ratio. At just 0.08%, FDIS ties XLY for the lowest fees, even coming in lower than Vanguard's consumer discretionary fund. All funds have a dividend yield of 1% or less.

Source: Seeking Alpha, 7 May 25

FDIS Holdings and Key Outlook Factors

FDIS and peer consumer discretionary funds, VCR and XLY, have the same top holdings of Amazon (AMZN), Tesla (TSLA), and Home Depot (HD). Despite FDIS's diversification, the fund is concentrated, with nearly 60% of its weight on its top 10 holdings. Only RSPD has significantly different top holdings due to its equal-weighted index.

Top 10 Holdings for FDIS and Competing Consumer Discretionary ETFs

FDIS - 262 holdings

VCR - 293 holdings

RSPD - 53 holdings

XLY - 51 holdings

Source: Multiple, compiled by author on 7 May 25

Because of the strong concentration in AMZN, TSLA, and HD, these top consumer discretionary companies will have a significant impact on FDIS's future performance. However, each company has concerning outlook factors discussed below. Additionally, the consumer discretionary sector as a whole is also demonstrating increased risk.

Amazon: Lower FCF and Tariff Risks

The first consideration factor is Amazon, FDIS's #1 holding, at over 21% weight. In its Q1 release, Amazon reported a free cash flow of just under $26B, down 48% YoY. While the company stated this was due to large capital expenditures on AI, Amazon has seen relatively flat net income in its North American segment since Q3 2024. Furthermore, tariff policies remain a major risk for Amazon. With over 70% of its products made in China, increased expenses will further impact its reduced free cash flow. Therefore, while Amazon may see sales increase over the long term, costs will continue weighing the company down. Additionally, Amazon's cloud services segment, AWS, also missed expectations in Q1 due to stiff competition with Microsoft's Azure cloud.

To add to these concerns, Amazon's valuation is also high, with a forward P/E over 30. Therefore, while AMZN's share price has fallen about 14% YTD, tariff policies and recession risks present a danger of its share price falling significantly further. Due to these concerns, AMZN does not contribute to a buy rating for FDIS overall.

Tesla: Increasing Competition and Struggling for Market Share

Tesla, the #2 holding of FDIS at over 14% weight, is also facing pressures. Notably, the company's Q1 2025 vehicle deliveries experienced a 13% decline. This drop in deliveries is predominantly due to increased competition from companies like China's BYD which has reduced Tesla sales in Europe and elsewhere around the world.

The pressures from competitors can best be measured in Tesla's reduced market share. In California, for example, Tesla used to command about 70% of the electric vehicle market share. However, this figure has been declining every quarter and was reported to be less than 50% in Q1 2025. These troublesome metrics have been part of the driving force behind a -31% YTD share price decline. However, TSLA's valuation remains undesirable with a forward P/E of 170, or 800% higher than its sector's median.

Home Depot: Mortgage Rate Uncertainty is a Concern

The third outlook factor is Home Depot, FDIS's #3 holding, at over 6% weight. While HD has seen a 10-year share price CAGR of over 12% with dividend growth of 15 years, there are risk factors ahead for its future growth. One key variable for Home Depot is the quantity of new home sales. Recently, new home sales have not increased due to elevated interest rates. With a mortgage rate around 7%, new home sales have remained muted. Fewer new home sales typically increase remodeling projects, thereby contributing to Home Depot's revenue.

However, the Federal Reserve recently stated that it is managing interest rates carefully to balance both inflation and unemployment. If a recession occurs and tariff-induced inflation is limited, interest rates may lower, resulting in an increase in new home sales. This dynamic would likely result in fewer existing home remodels, and therefore lower sales for Home Depot. Even before this recent data, Home Depot had seen mediocre fundamental metrics, including a YoY EBITDA growth of just 1.4%. While it is too early to know the impact of tariffs and therefore interest rate changes, there is enough risk to warrant caution.

Current Valuation

FDIS has slightly underperformed the broader market, as measured by the S&P 500, and XLY over the past year. XLY, the least diversified fund compared, outperformed FDIS predominantly due to its higher concentration on top companies discussed in this article, such as TSLA, which saw a share price jump of 55% over the past year.

FDIS currently has a more attractive valuation than peer funds VCR and XLY as measured by P/E and P/B ratios. However, given the risk factors discussed for each of these funds' top-weighted companies, FDIS does not warrant a buy rating at this time.

Valuation Metrics for FDIS and Peer Consumer Discretionary Funds

FDIS

VCR

RSPD

XLY

P/E ratio

23.91

22.30

P/B ratio

4.45

Source: Compiled by Author from Multiple Sources, 7 May 25

Risks to Investors

FDIS has a 5-year beta value of 1.40, indicating greater volatility for the market overall. However, during the past five years, aside from the COVID pandemic, the U.S. economy has yielded generally positive performance. Should a recession occur, FDIS will see high volatility and significant risk for investors. Warning signs are already emerging that point towards this risk. For example, real disposable personal income per capita, or RDPI, in the United States saw a decline in Q1 2025 according to FRED economic data. This represents the first time in a decade that RDPI dropped from the previous year.

This decline is worrisome for consumer discretionary companies like Amazon, Tesla, and Home Depot, which rely on disposable income to drive sales. The U.S. personal savings rate has also hit a low, below 5%, meaning that in the event of a recession, the consumer discretionary sector may be hit particularly hard.

Concluding Summary

FDIS is a low-fee fund that effectively captures a diverse mix of U.S. consumer discretionary companies. While top holdings like Amazon, Tesla, and Home Depot have historically performed well, there are several current risk factors impacting their outlook. Reduced disposable income for U.S. consumers is already showing some negative signs including reduced sales for FDIS's top holdings. Furthermore, tariff policies and interest rates present other unknown risks for the consumer discretionary sector.
